activities.markdown:
## The Piano Keyboard: A Map of Western Pitch

The piano keyboard is the clearest, most visual representation of pitch in all of Western music. Even if you never play piano, **understanding the keyboard is essential** for every musician — it gives you a physical map where all scales, chords, and intervals become visible.

### The Numbers

A full-size piano keyboard has:
- **88 keys total**
- **52 white keys**
- **36 black keys**
- **7.25 octaves** of range

For theory purposes, you need to understand the repeating pattern within a single **octave** — the same pattern tiles across the entire keyboard.

### The Musical Alphabet

The white keys use the 7-letter musical alphabet: **A B C D E F G**. After G, the alphabet repeats from A. These seven letters correspond to the seven distinct pitch classes in Western music (called the **diatonic** notes).

### The Octave Pattern

One octave = **12 keys** (7 white + 5 black). This 12-key pattern repeats identically from one end of the keyboard to the other. The pitch name tells you *what* note it is; an octave number (C4, C5, C6) tells you *which octave*.

### The Black Key Groups: Your Landmark System

Black keys come in alternating groups:
- **Groups of 2** and **groups of 3**, repeating across the entire keyboard

This grouping is your navigation system:
- **C** is always the white key **immediately to the left of a group of 2 black keys**
- **F** is always the white key **immediately to the left of a group of 3 black keys**

With C and F as landmarks, you can find any other note without counting from the beginning of the keyboard each time.

## Navigating the Keyboard: Octaves and Middle C

### Middle C (C4)

The reference point for the entire keyboard is **middle C**, officially designated **C4** in scientific pitch notation. It is:
- The C closest to the exact center of a standard 88-key keyboard
- Located on the first ledger line below the treble clef staff
- Located on the first ledger line above the bass clef staff
- The starting point from which octave numbers are counted

### Octave Numbering

Octave numbers increment at every C:
- **C4** = middle C
- **C5** = one octave above middle C
- **C3** = one octave below middle C
- **A4** = 440 Hz, the international tuning standard (the A above middle C)

### White Key Names in Order

Starting from any C and going up to the next C:
> **C — D — E — F — G — A — B — C**

Each adjacent pair of white keys is either a **whole step** (with a black key between them) or a **half step** (no black key between them). The natural half steps on the white keys are:
- **E to F** (no black key between E and F)
- **B to C** (no black key between B and C)

All other adjacent white keys have a black key between them.

### Why Every Musician Needs This

Scales, chords, and intervals all become transparent when mapped to a keyboard:
- You can **see** a major scale as a pattern of steps
- You can **see** a chord as a cluster of keys
- You can **see** why C major has no sharps or flats (all white keys)
- You can **see** interval distances as physical distances between keys

activities.question: How many keys are in one octave on the piano keyboard?
activities.explanation: One octave consists of 12 keys: 7 white and 5 black. This 12-key pattern (12 half steps) repeats across the entire piano keyboard.

activities.question: The note C can always be found in what position relative to the black keys?
choices: To the right of a group of 3 black keys
To the left of a group of 2 black keys
Between two groups of black keys
To the left of a group of 3 black keys
activities.explanation: C is always the white key immediately to the left of a group of 2 black keys. This landmark pattern repeats across the entire keyboard and is the quickest way to find any C without counting every key.

activities.question: What is the standard scientific pitch designation for middle C?
activities.explanation: Middle C is officially C4 in scientific pitch notation. It is the C closest to the center of a standard 88-key keyboard, and the reference point from which octave numbers count up and down.
